source: Petitions. Members of the Federated Malay States Railway Clerical Service on the subject of the revised 1938 clerical scheme__CO273:660:3
filename: Petitions. Members of the Federated Malay States Railway Clerical Service on the subject of the revised 1938 clerical scheme__CO273:660:3.txt
summary:
- **Core topic/purpose:** Petition/memorials from members of the **Federated Malay States (FMS) Railway Clerical Service** seeking changes to the **revised/amended 1938 Railway Clerical Scheme** (pay scales, promotion rules, examinations, ratios of posts, and pension/provident fund terms).  
- **Geography & time period:** **Federated Malay States, Malaya**; scheme effective **retroactively from 1 Jan 1938**; correspondence and meetings mainly **1938–1940** (including a key meeting **16 May 1940** and Colonial Office handling in **Dec 1940**).  
- **Key actors:** Petitioners represented by the **Government Junior Officers’ Association (FMS)** (President **R. V. Karlakandan**, Hon. Secretary **V. George**); FMS Government officials including **General Manager, FMS Railways**, **A. T. Newboult** (Under Secretary), **E. E. F. Pretty** (Acting Under Secretary); High Commissioner **S. W. Jones** (and references to former High Commissioner **Sir Shenton Thomas**); addressed to UK Colonial Office/Secretary of State (**Malcolm MacDonald**) and **Lord Lloyd**.  
- **Main requests/proposals by clerical staff:** abolish the **Lower Division**; address **stagnation** by promoting **Class III** clerks (including a request for **retrospective promotion from 1 Jan 1937**); maintain/restore prior **senior clerical examination** arrangements (objecting to written competitive exams/Promotion Board procedures); increase **Class I** ratio (requested **20%**); raise maxima (**Class II to $175**, **Class I to $225**); increase/promote to **Special Class** by ratio (requested up to **10%**, later **5%** mentioned); reinstate/create more **superscale** posts; restore **pension privileges** instead of compulsory **Railway Provident Fund** contributions.  
- **Government/High Commissioner decisions & concessions noted:** refused major structural changes (e.g., **not abolishing Lower Division**, **not raising Class I to 20%**, **not restoring pensions for post-1936 entrants**, and generally no further modifications beyond those already made); concessions included raising **Lower Division minimum salary to $40/month** (from $35) and adding a **Special Grade $110–A5–$150/month**; allowing **supernumerary Higher Division posts** to promote suitable Lower Division staff after **3 years** (a palliative where vacancies were scarce); dropping the **written examination** (concession aligned with earlier General Clerical Service handling); raising **Class II maximum to $160/month** (not $175).  
- **Notable ratios/numbers cited:** agreement that **Class I posts** be **not less than 15% of the total clerical establishment** (including Lower Division), with an agreed application for **1 Jan 1938–16 May 1940** based on actual strength at **16 May 1940**; dispute over earlier interpretation of 1934 scheme (15% of Higher Division vs total); claim that **24 superscale appointments** were held by **Asiatic staff** (15 promoted from Lower Clerical Service), countering petitioners’ claim of only three; annexure figures (as presented by petitioners) show Special Class & superscale proportions: **General Clerical 6.8%**, **Posts & Telegraphs 6.9%**, **Railway 3.6%**.  
- **Pensions/provident fund detail:** **Railway Provident Fund** applied to entrants **on/after 1 July 1936** (petition text also references **7½% salary contribution after 10 years’ service**); government position: earlier entrants retained old non-contributory pension terms, and new entrants were informed at recruitment; fund approval referenced to **10 Feb 1936** despatch (J. H. Thomas).
